projects.title: Management Innovation Pilots
projects.mission: This is a continuation of the initial FY 2024 Management Innovation Pilot (H.R. 118-121) and seeks to improve warfighter lethality and DoW efficiency by using data, analytics, and AI to modernize business practices across CDAO and DoW.

projects.title: Acquisition Ecosystem
projects.mission: Funding for Acquisition Ecosystem leverages digital and AI technologies to improve acquisition processes and provides training to the acquisition workforce on how to procure digital and AI technologies more effectively. Streamline commercial data and AI company access to DoW contracts via the Tradewinds Acquisition Ecosystem Solution Marketplace and Commercial Solutions Opportunity/Other Transaction Authority vehicles to rapidly field data and AI cutting-edge capabilities to warfighters. The goal is to develop, sustain, and share across the acquisition workforce acquisition approaches that enable greater agility, speed, and mission utility when procuring digital and software-based technologies.

programElements.peName: Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer (CDAO) - MIP
programElements.mission: The Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer (CDAO) supports the Department's initiatives to build enduring advantage and build a resilient Joint Force and defense ecosystem. The CDAO is responsible for strengthening and integrating data, artificial intelligence, and digital solutions in the Department in support of the interim National Defense Strategy and Section 1513 of the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) for FY 2023. The functions of the CDAO are as follows: lead and oversee the DoW’s strategy development and policy formulation for data, analytics, and AI; break down barriers to data and AI adoption within DoW institutional processes; create enabling digital infrastructure and services that support Components’ development and deployment of data, analytics, AI, and digital-enabled solutions; selectively scale proven digital and AI-enabled solutions focused on enterprise and joint use cases; and surge digital services for rapid response to crises and emergent challenges. The CDAO will continue priority projects that align to the mission. This includes expanding the AI integration between the DoW and Intelligence Community (IC); enterprise data repository; establishing a responsible AI ecosystem; executing the AI and Data Accelerator (ADA) initiative; and developing a Data, Analytics, and AI Adoption Strategy. These various lines of effort will support the overarching mission of accelerating the Department’s adoption of data, analytics, and AI to preserve decision advantage across the Joint Force. The CDAO’s DoW and IC Integration Division was chartered to create joint interoperability and to harness and scale AI across the DoW and IC jointly, especially within the Defense Intelligence Enterprise. The organizational purpose is to improve coordination and interoperability between DoW and the IC, minimize duplication while maximizing a common approach, and develop an innovated joint AI operational concept and capability, where appropriate. This funding is assigned to support algorithm development, data preparation, and integration experimentation to create joint DoW and IC capabilities. Further details are classified and available upon request. PE 0604124D8Z: Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligenc... UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 529 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 6 R-1 Line #105

projects.title: Intelligence Support
projects.mission: The CDAO’s DoW and IC Integration Division was chartered to create joint interoperability and to harness and scale AI across the DoW and IC jointly, especially within the Defense Intelligence Enterprise. The organizational purpose is to improve coordination and interoperability between DoW and the IC, minimize duplication while maximizing a common approach, and develop an innovated joint AI operational concept and capability, where appropriate. This funding is assigned to support algorithm development, data preparation, and integration experimentation to create joint DoW and IC capabilities. Further details are classified and available upon request.

programElements.mission: The Advanced Manufacturing Components and Prototypes (AMCAP) program is established to validate the build and/or expansion of advanced manufacturing infrastructure and conduct research, develop, test, and evaluation (RDT&E) to facilitate development and transition of promising advanced manufacturing technologies to higher manufacturing readiness levels (MRL) via component and prototype development. Funding for this program is focused on requirements emerging from the Defense Biomanufacturing Strategy, which is guiding the execution of the Distributed Bioindustrial Manufacturing Program (DBIMP) – formerly known as the Manufacturing Enabled by Modular and Reusable (MEMBR) assets initiative investments to scale up domestic manufacturing of bioindustrial products. The project under this program will cover the full range of technologies critical to the Department of War (DoW) and manufacturing technologies with dual-use commercial applications to support the industrial base that will supply the DoW.

projects.title: Advanced Manufacturing Components
projects.mission: This project will mature manufacturing processes to support the transition of advanced manufacturing components and prototypes to address warfighter needs. With initial emphasis in the bioindustrial manufacturing area, the project will advance the scale up production of molecules of interest. Through the biotechnology Distributed Bioindustrial Manufacturing Program (DBIMP) – formerly known as the Manufacturing Enabled by Modular Bioindustrial & Reusable (MEMBR) assets initiative - the project will establish a network of flexible manufacturing facilities to scale-up promising biotechnology capabilities for integration into DoW missions.

programElements.mission: Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer (CDAO) are spearheading the Department of War's transformation into an AI-enabled enterprise by focusing on warfighting, intelligence, and enterprise-wide AI deployment. The core of this strategy revolves around a series of pioneering "Pace-Setting Projects" (PSPs). adoption, the Enterprise Agents PSP provides a playbook for deploying AI workflows. Underpinning all these efforts are two foundational enablers: the new War Data Platform (WDP) for standardized data access and the "GenAI.mil" initiative to scale AI tools across the force. In parallel, Autonomy.mil will provide similar standardization for autonomy platform development across the DoW. The CDAO will champion this transformation as a "Wartime CDAO," cutting through bureaucracy to rapidly deliver these capabilities. CDAO is also evolving its AI model assessment to evaluate future models on a wide variety of use cases including performance, testing, ethics, and security compliance. The FY 2027 request for Alpha 1 includes $969,825 thousand of discretionary and $500,000 thousand of mandatory for a total of $1,469,825. The mandatory funds Alpha 1 which is to support pace setting projects and generative AI for all government. The FY 2027 mandatory funds continue efforts from FY 2026 to support the Artificial Intelligence Strategy for the Department of War. The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Alpha-1 includes $395,882 thousand of discretionary and $250,000 thousand of mandatory for a total of $645,882 thousand. PE 0604133D8Z: Alpha-1 Development Activities UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 815 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 12 R-1 Line #145

projects.title: Alpha-1 Development
projects.mission: Alpha-1 provided enterprise services and capabilities to enable AI efforts across DoW organizations and mission areas. Note: Funding related to P-Code: 081 - Alpha-1 Development was realigned to P-Code 773 within this Program Element in 2026; no additional requests under this sub- project exist.

projects.title: AI & Autonomy Enterprise Enablers
projects.mission: Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer (CDAO) are spearheading the Department of War's transformation into an AI-enabled enterprise by focusing on warfighting, intelligence, and enterprise-wide AI deployment. The core of this strategy revolves around a series of pioneering "Pace-Setting Projects" (PSPs). For warfighting, initiatives like Swarm Forge, Agent Network, and Ender's Foundry are accelerating AI in novel combat methods, battle management, and simulations. In the intelligence domain, Open Arsenal and Project Grant aim to shorten the intelligence-to-action timeline and transform deterrence strategies. To drive enterprise-wide adoption, the Enterprise Agents PSP provides a playbook for deploying AI workflows. Underpinning all these efforts are two foundational enablers: the new War Data Platform (WDP) for standardized data access and the "GenAI.mil" initiative to scale AI tools across the force. In parallel, Autonomy.mil will provide similar standardization for autonomy platform development across the DoW. The CDAO will champion this transformation as a "Wartime CDAO," cutting through bureaucracy to rapidly deliver these capabilities.

programElements.mission: Nuclear Matters, System Development & Demonstration addresses the need to defend and deter against weapons of mass destruction threats and to safeguard personnel, prevent unauthorized access to equipment, installations, material, and documents, and to safeguard the foregoing against espionage, sabotage, damage, and theft. This program oversees advanced engineering development and rapid fielding throughout the Department of War (DoW) for an integrated and systemic approach to develop material solutions. The Physical Security Enterprise and Analysis Program (PSEAP) avoids duplication of effort, ensures systems integration, and promotes interoperability and sustainability. The material solutions either (a) lead to a Program of Record, (b) become technology insertions into existing programs; or (c) advance to being a certified Commercial/Government off-the-shelf product. The PSEAP advances enterprise-level physical security RDT&E solutions to reduce risk created by current and emerging threat by analyzing, researching, developing, demonstrating, and evaluating interoperable solutions to transition capability to warfighters. Collaboration with DoW Components, other government agencies, and the international community to prioritize and close capability gaps is a top priority of the PSEAP and the physical security community. National Technical Nuclear Forensics (NTNF) mission requirements and responsibilities derive from multiple source documents. Per National Security Presidential Memorandum 35 (NSPM 35), the DoW leads the U.S. Government (USG) post-detonation NTNF capability. NSPM 35 also directs that the US will maintain and advance a robust NTNF capability to detect, identify, locate, and characterize the attribution of the source of an actual or attempted nuclear or radiological attack in all environments, to include underground, underwater, in the atmosphere, and space. Per DoW Directive S-2060.04, the Office of the Undersecretary of War for Acquisition & Sustainment (OUSW(A&S)) is the office responsible for developing and leading the DoW’s NTNF capabilities. Per the 2022 Nuclear Posture Review (NPR) Direction, greater investment in NTNF tools is required to ensure they remain responsive to the threat, and thus scientifically credible and internationally accepted to hold states facilitating acts of nuclear terrorism accountable. The FY 2022 – FY 2026 Nuclear Forensics and Attribution Strategic (NFAS) Plan requires an NTNF capability to collect, analyze, and evaluate both air and ground post-nuclear detonation samples at accelerated directed timelines. The DoW mission to collect, analyze, and evaluate post- detonation prompt signals and nuclear debris is critical to our national defense and security to ensuring the USG can identify the source of nuclear material and hold those involved or supporting an attack accountable. Internal and independent assessments indicate that new capabilities are needed to sustain an effective deterrent against an unattributed nuclear attack and meet the challenges of future threats. projects.title: Nuclear and Conventional Physical
projects.mission: The Physical Security Enterprise and Analysis Program (PSEAP) pursues the development of nuclear and conventional physical security materiel solutions in response to the stated needs and requirements of the Combatant Commands and Military Services. This program leverages commonalities in physical security requirements in order to closely balance and integrate the needs of users. The PSEAP is responsible for avoiding duplication of effort, ensuring systems integration, and promoting interoperability and sustainability. The materiel solutions either (a) lead to a Program of Record, (b) become technology insertions into existing programs; or (c) advance to being a certified Commercial/Government off-the-shelf product.

projects.title: National Technical Nuclear Forensics
projects.mission: Per National Security Presidential Memorandum 35, the Department of War (DoW) leads the United States Government (USG) post-detonation National Technical Nuclear Forensics (NTNF) capability and directs the U.S. to maintain and advance NTNF capabilities leading to the attribution of the source of an actual or attempted nuclear attack in all environments. Per the 2022 Nuclear Posture Review, greater investment in NTNF tools is required to ensure they remain responsive to the threat. Per DoDD S-2060.04, OUSW(A&S) is the DoW office responsible for DoW’s NTNF capabilities. Per the FY 2022 – FY 2026 Nuclear Forensics Analysis System Plan, accelerated timelines are required for the NTNF capability to collect, analyze, and evaluate both air and ground post-nuclear detonation samples. This Program Element is the only DoW Research, Development, Test and Evaluation program focused on System Development & Demonstration for NTNF capabilities. Collecting, analyzing, and evaluating post-detonation prompt signals and nuclear debris is critical to ensure the USG can identify the source of nuclear material and holding those involved or supporting an attack accountable is critical to our national defense and security. Swift and accurate forensic and attribution capabilities are vital to supporting the President and Secretary of Defense in developing an appropriate, and timely, national response to a nuclear event and to prevent future attacks. An effective NTNF capability ensures potential adversaries, or those who support them, know that they will be held accountable if they use proxies or other non-traditional delivery (e.g., false-flag operations) of nuclear weapons against the U.S., U.S. interests, or allies. Both internal and independent studies indicate that continued improvement to the USG's NTNF capabilities is needed to sustain a credible deterrent against an attempted or actual nuclear attack. Additionally, this program sustains perishable U.S. technical expertise at the operational DoW laboratories required to respond to a post-detonation NTNF event. The DoW's laboratory capability in this area is limited by capacity and technical expertise. Increased support of the DoW’s NTNF mission is crucial to prevent attrition of current capabilities and knowledge base, ensure that this critical and unique deterrence capability is not lost, putting the security of the nation and the ability to deter specific kinds of nuclear attack at risk, and meeting a higher standard of timeliness and confidence as directed.

programElements.mission: Chemical and biological weapons (CBW)-based threats continue to evolve in response to changing geopolitics and emerging technologies. Each of the nations that are deemed as threats to United States (U.S.) national security are believed to have and/or be developing chemical and/or biological weapons. The Department of War (DoW) must be prepared to eliminate these threats during and/or after the execution of a contingency operation or in support of a diplomatic breakthrough, enabling the U.S. to achieve peace through strength and strengthen deterrence. Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC) and Biological Weapons Convention (BWC) obligations and DoW strategic guidance demand the Department maintain a complete and enduring capability to assess and destroy CBW, regardless of location or environment. In accordance with the CWC and BWC, the U.S. must be prepared to “destroy [chemical/biological] weapons…in its possession or…under its jurisdiction or control.” It is critical for the DoW to match CBW-based threats by possessing the capabilities to directly disable or defeat WMD threats prior to use. This capability must provide operational flexibility that enables early action and expedient destruction, from small-scale disablement and defeat in a non-permissive or austere environment to large-scale destruction of a foreign CBW stockpile in a post-contingency or permissive environment. These capabilities will positively influence deterrence and project Peace Through Strength. Our current capabilities are inadequate to support these requirements, and we are currently unable to invest in the development and/or improvement of capabilities to eliminate CBW on the scale required. The Department must be proactive in ensuring we are prepared to eliminate known and expected CBW threats before adversary use against U.S. military or civilian personnel at home or abroad or our allies. The Chemical and Biological Weapons Elimination (CBWE) Research and Development Activities (RDAs) support the Department’s initiatives to rebuild our military and reestablish deterrence by ensuring a complete and enduring capability to assess and destroy chemical and biological weapons (CBW), regardless of location or operational environment. This program addresses Joint Force priorities, tasks, and requirements. Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 funding is for the advanced development of chemical weapons destruction technologies in support of joint warfighter requirements. The CBWE portfolio provides material solutions that enable DoW to prevent adversary acquisition, transfer, deployment, and use of CBW as well as meet U.S. Chemical Warfare Convention (CWC) treaty obligations. programElements.mission: In support of the Interim National Defense Strategy, the Strategic Capabilities Office (SCO) develops, demonstrates, and transitions operationally impactful capabilities and effects to shape and counter emerging threats and increase the lethality of the Joint Force in contested environments. The SCO combines innovation with concepts of operation to develop novel capabilities solving critical national security challenges in partnership with the Services, Defense Agencies, Combatant Commands (CCMDS), Joint Chiefs of Staff, Intelligence Community, and the Office of the Secretary of War (OSW). The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Advanced Innovative Technologies 0604250D8Z includes $770,844 thousand of discretionary, and $1,002,000 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $1,772,844 thousand.

projects.title: Advanced Innovative Technologies
projects.mission: The Strategic Capabilities Office (SCO) develops, demonstrates, and transitions operationally impactful capabilities and effects to shape and counter emerging threats and increase the lethality of the Joint Force in contested environments. The SCO combines innovation with concepts of operation and information management to develop novel capabilities solving critical national security challenges in partnership with the Services, Defense Agencies, Combatant Commands (CCMDS), Joint Chiefs of Staff, Intelligence Community, and the Office of the Secretary of War (OSW). SCO's FY 2026 Pacific Deterrence Initiative (PDI) spending under Exercises, Training, Experimentation, and Innovation includes $639.296 million discretionary and $167.000 million mandatory for a total of $806.296, an increase of $150.786 million from FY 2025, with five new capability projects transitioning into Execution in FY 2026.

programElements.mission: This program provides microelectronics development and prototyping activities that support the 2026 National Defense Strategy Line of Effort to supercharge the United States Defense Industrial Base through building out capacity, empowering innovators and adopting new advancements in technology, guided by the Department’s policy on Assured Access to Trusted Microelectronics, and 10 USC 4128, Joint Federated Assurance Center. Microelectronics and their supporting supply chains face unprecedented and constantly changing and evolving security risks that threaten national security and the performance of Department of War (DoW) systems and technologies. Microelectronics products and services for DoW systems are most often obtained from a global supply chain. This requires the Department to have multiple options to maintain assured access to trusted microelectronics by managing risks from supply chain disruptions and malicious activity during all phases of design, manufacturing, packaging, test, and distribution. Microelectronics are a critical enabler of military systems to ensure the Joint Force is second to none. This program delivers trusted and assured microelectronics to support the lethal force through: Providing access to state-of-the-art (SOTA) microelectronics design, emulation tools, and intellectual property (IP) libraries; advanced fabrication nodes, and advanced packaging and test capabilities; and commercial device implementation and development of devices to strengthen the lethal force of the Department. projects.title: Access to State-of-the-Art (SOTA)
projects.mission: This project establishes strategic partnerships with commercial SOTA domestic foundries to develop a data-driven, risk- based approaches to supply chain protection and develops the assured access, secure design, and manufacturing capability of advanced microelectronics technology and electronic components. This project funds microelectronics software assurance (SwA) support to DoW programs and supports the activities of the Joint Federated Assurance Center (JFAC), codified in the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) for Fiscal Year 2025, Sec 922, to support the assurance needs of the DoW by ensuring, pursuant to policies related to hardware and software assurance and supply chain risk management, that the software and hardware developed, acquired, maintained, and used by the Department are free from intentional and unintentional vulnerability during the life-cycle of development and deployment of assured, trustworthy defense systems. Projects are transitioning technologies into DoW systems and platforms, ensuring access to multiple onshore commercial microelectronics facilities, establishing secure design capabilities, implementing an evidence-based approach to supply chain protection, and developing technologies for situational awareness of the global microelectronics supply chain. This project builds the foundation for updating and strengthening DoW assurance methods and standards with industry to develop data driven evidence-based practices.

projects.title: Access to Advanced Packaging and
projects.mission: Advanced packaging provides necessary performance improvements, size, weight, power, and cost (SWAP-C) benefits, and unique opportunities to disaggregate DoW requirements into smaller and lower cost chiplets while maximally leveraging SOTA commercial technologies. However, domestic advanced packaging capabilities are limited and the heterogeneous integrated (HI) chiplet ecosystem is nascent. This project is a collaborative effort among the DoW, the Defense Industrial Base (DIB), and domestic commercial industry to leverage and enhance existing capabilities to develop self-sustaining advanced packaging and test solutions that meet DoW needs.

projects.title: DOW Unique Needs RADHARD
projects.mission: The DoW relies on a small number of specialty suppliers to develop and acquire the application specific integrated circuits (ASICs) and specialized or modified commercial components required to satisfy military needs for radiation hardened digital and analog microelectronics. By partnering in the maturation of SOTA material sources, foundries, and packaging facilities, the DoW is aligning commercial practices with unique needs for performance and functionality and encouraging open access design to stimulate innovation and drive affordability. Expanding the manufacturing capability and capacity of facilities for fabrication, packaging, and testing are all enablers to ensuring availability of radiation hardened microelectronics.

projects.title: ME U.S. ME Dominance-Dev
projects.mission: This project promotes microelectronics innovation and creates an evidence-based assured microelectronics pipeline and strategic partnerships for assuring field programmable gate array (FPGA) devices, supply chain awareness and security, and workforce development. It will strengthen workforce expertise and ensure DoW has access to the next generation of advanced technology by utilizing an evidence-based assurance approach. This approach enables management of microelectronics security that is commensurate with program risk throughout the product pipeline and maintain the United States as the global source for high-end, secure, and reliable microelectronics components. In addition, this project will develop new data driven risk-based assurance methodologies for supply chain protection to strengthen security while improving access, without exposing sensitive intellectual property (IP) to the foundry and requiring post-manufacture validation of foundry products. The project will develop evidence- based assured design concepts in manufactured systems, enabling a formal risk-based approach to protection techniques.

projects.title: Software Assurance
projects.mission: This project funds microelectronics software assurance (SwA) support to DoW programs and supports the activities of the Joint Federated Assurance Center (JFAC), codified in the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) for Fiscal Year 2025, Sec 922, to support the assurance needs of the DoW by ensuring, pursuant to policies related to hardware and software assurance and supply chain risk management, that the software and hardware developed, acquired, maintained, and used by the Department are free from intentional and unintentional vulnerability during the life-cycle of development and deployment of assured, trustworthy defense systems.

programElements.mission: The Rapid Prototyping Program (RPP) supports the Department's initiatives to Defend the Homeland, Build Sustainable and Long-Term Advantage, and Build a Resilient Joint Force and Defense Ecosystem. RPP supports the rapid development of prototypes within 12-to-24 months to address joint urgent and emergent needs identified through ideation with the Joint Staff, Combatant Commands, or Secretary of War guidance. Prototype requirements are developed using threat-informed physics-based mission analyses and are evaluated under the Office of the Assistant Secretary of War for Mission Capabilities’ (OASW(MC)) campaign of experimentation, resulting in military utility assessments. RPP supports the maturation of joint prototypes across the Department (including those developed by the Strategic Capabilities Office (SCO), Defense Innovation Unit (DIU), Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), and the Services (TRL 5-6)) that have successfully demonstrated a required capability but have not been independently assessed in an interoperable, system of systems architecture. Overarching program goals include modernization of cross-cutting joint technology areas, providing fieldable end-to-end mission capabilities for joint application, informing disparate programs of record, and delivering capabilities more quickly than traditional acquisition. RPP develops prototypes that reduce technical and integration risk across the Services and accelerate capabilities to programs of record and joint, rapid experimentation. RPP project selection aligns to strategic guidance including priority joint mission threads and the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering’s (USW(R&E)) critical technology areas. RPP rapidly develops and fields cross-cutting, joint prototype capabilities demonstrated in an operational environment to inform DoW and Service leadership. In FY 2026, RPP organized projects by focus areas aligned to specific, persistent capability gaps. Aligning the program to Secretary of War priorities, the following focus areas will allow Prototyping and Experimentation to rapidly respond to urgent and emergent requirements at the speed of relevance. Focus areas include: Kill Web Interoperability: The processes by which Joint Force Commanders plan, direct, coordinate, and control forces to accomplish assigned missions and enable joint kill webs. Resilient Communications: The ability to maintain hardened mission-capable systems that allow for secure freedom of communications in contested electromagnetic spectrum environments. This includes alternate position, navigation, and timing. PE 0604331D8Z: Rapid Prototyping Program UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 615 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 32 R-1 Line #111

projects.title: Rapid Prototyping Program
projects.mission: The Rapid Prototyping Program (RPP) develops prototypes to deliver capabilities, inform requirements, and bridge the gap between RDT&E activities and fieldable solutions. RPP facilitates and accelerates joint, cross-cutting prototyping efforts within the Services and Defense Agencies. This program has the agility to select, fund, and implement projects in the year of execution as new opportunities or threats emerge. In consultation with the Service Science and Technology (S&T) executives, selected projects generally receive a single year of funding to accelerate capability transition to Services’ and Agencies’ programs of record. Projects deemed critical by the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering (USW(R&E)), the Joint Staff, and the Combatant Commands receive higher amounts of funding across multiple years. Planned funding supports the Joint Warfighting Concept (JWC), the Office of the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering's (OUSW(R&E)) critical technology areas, and Service and Agency needs to enable rapid response to emergent and time-sensitive threats.

programElements.mission: The Defense Innovation Unit (DIU) mission is to strengthen U.S. national security by accelerating the adoption of commercial technology and externally developed products--connecting American entrepreneurs and commercial technology companies to Department needs at speed. DIU serves as the Department's center of excellence for commercial technology adoption and product transition, available to Portfolio Acquisition Executives and program offices across the Department. DIU is the Department's primary interface with the nontraditional and commercial ecosystem for rapid product adoption and transition. In support of the Secretary of War’s directive to transform the Defense Innovation Ecosystem to Accelerate Warfighting Advantage, DIU will: 1) Focus on high technologies that produce overmatch domination; 2) Focus on technologies that reduce the number of Soldiers, Sailors, Airmen, Marines, and Guardians in harm’s way; 3) Focus on capabilities that can scale quickly and dramatically reduce costs. Through a competitive prototype process, DIU identifies and provides access to technology companies and products on behalf of DoW organizations. Additionally, DIU executes projects to leverage commercial sector technology analogous to military applications thereby increasing dual-use technology agility for the DoW. DIU Prototyping funds facilitate the award of projects that can augment commercial technologies, existing government-owned capabilities, or concepts for defense application.

programElements.peName: Department of Defense (DoD) Unmanned Systems Common Develo
programElements.mission: FY 2027 request for Department of War (DoW) Unmanned Systems Common Development includes $2,024 thousands of discretionary and $34,627 thousands of mandatory for a total of $36.651 thousand. The mandatory funds will provide additional support for Unmanned Systems Development, Integration, and Integration Support missions, allowing OUSW(A&S) to implement the Acquisition Transformation Strategy for unmanned systems across the department. Funds will also support the blue list marketplace that supports industries in development of UAS systems and warfighter procurement of secure systems. The FY 2027 funds are different from the FY 2026 mandatory funds that support continued development of multiple unmanned air systems, Task Force Baker operationalization activities, and test range and target costs. Results from these development efforts drive appropriate LRIP decisions as platform designs are iterated. These funds are executed in close coordination with service POR strategies and budgets. The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Department of War (DoW) Unmanned Systems Common Development includes $11,971 thousands of discretionary and $1,000,000 thousands of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $1,011,971 thousands. This program supports the Department's initiatives to Restoring Warrior Ethos, Rebuilding Military Capabilities and Re-establishing Deterrence. The Department of War (DoW) Unmanned System Common Development Program allows the Office of the Under Secretary of War for Acquisition and Sustainment (OUSW(A&S)) to support both the near-term and long-term advancement of DoW unmanned systems capabilities. These efforts are in direct support of both the letter and the intent of the following directives (1) Executive Order 14307 on Unleashing Drone Dominance signed on June 6, 2025 to enhance the capabilities of U.S. warfighters and support the American drone industry and (2) the Secretary of War memorandum on Unleashing Drone Dominance signed on July 10, 2025 to rebuild our military Unmanned Aerial Systems capabilities. This includes the development of common technologies, standards, concepts, and guidance that can be applied across all Military Services. This effort focuses on the development of technologies and concepts that support unmanned systems of all sizes and modalities to include the operation of DoW Small Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS) both in the U.S. National Airspace and in an expeditionary environment. Key aspects of this effort includes (1) PE 0604400D8Z: Department of Defense (DoD) Unmanned Sys... UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 655 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 21 R-1 Line #114

projects.title: Unmanned Systems Development
projects.mission: FY 2027 request for Unmanned Systems Development supports the Department’s unmanned system development goals of making available commercial unmanned systems and components for DoW procurement and operations that are safe, secure, interoperable, and effective on the modern battlefield. This effort includes: (1) identifying and mitigating unmanned system related cyber and operations security threats, (2) developing standards, concepts, and guidance that support the Department’s procurement and operation of unmanned systems, and (3) facilitating the acquisition of modern commercially developed unmanned systems for DoW use that are sourced from trusted supply chains and are cyber-secure.

projects.title: Unmanned Systems Integration
projects.mission: FY 2027 funding for Unmanned Systems Integration includes $693 thousand of discretionary funds which supports the Department’s unmanned system integration goals of facilitating access and integration of unmanned systems into military and civilian environments in a safe and effective manner. This effort includes: (1) the safe and effective integration of UXS within civil and military environments and (2) the development of standards, concepts, and guidance to support this integration. In the near- term, this project is focused on the development of concepts and prototype systems that support the DoW UXS integration mission and includes the execution of various evaluation events that integrate military UXS within air, ground, and maritime environments. This effort also includes the development and integration of current airspace access and integration system initiatives to include UAS Drone Zone and UAS Traffic Management (UTM). The long-term goal of this project is to establish the ability for DoW UAS and UGV systems to operate beyond visual line of sight (BVLOS) safely, effectively, and routinely within civil and military environments. The results from this effort will be shared with federal partners, to include the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), in order to help shape the development of UAS airspace access and integration related concepts, technology, and rulemaking.

projects.title: Unmanned Systems Integration
projects.mission: FY 2027 request for Unmanned Systems Integration Support supports the Department’s unmanned system integration goals of establishing and overseeing coordination, agreements, guidance, and standards that enable the DoW’s operation of unmanned systems in military and civilian environments. This effort includes leading multiple Joint and Interagency forums focused on identifying, prioritizing, and executing tasks aimed at overcoming challenges associated with the successful development, fielding, and operation of DoW unmanned systems.

programElements.mission: The mission of the Economic Defense Unit (EDU) is to support some of the Department of War's (DoW) Economic Competition activities by providing analysis, recommendations, insights, and other support to ensure alignment with DoW objectives and in support of the DoW’s National Defense Strategy, National Military Strategy, and National Defense Industrial Strategy. Additionally, the office will develop requirements for Economic Competition activities that may include access, basing, and overflight; countering mobilization of adversaries; countering defense industrial base activities by adversaries; and ensuring access for the United States to critical materials and capabilities. It will also provide economic analysis and advisory input on DoW contracting and other commercial approaches meeting criteria developed by the Director of EDU, and sponsor and conduct regular tabletop exercises and activities to assess the economic impacts of DoW decisions during crisis and conflicts. The EDU's economic weapon system will integrate economic competition workflows into a single system in order to (1) Enable data-driven coordination across economic competition activities; (2) Improve collaboration via recurring system usage by EDU; (3) Establish an enduring operating model for data acquisition and integration; (4) Support planning and execution of economic competition activities at scale. The FY 2027 request for EDU includes $403,903 thousand of discretionary and $189,378 thousand of mandatory for a total of $593,281 thousand. The mandatory funds EDU's investment program which will make strategic equity and control investments in companies that align with its mission. This includes securing access to critical minerals and materials, enhancing access, basing and overflight, countering adversary mobilization and countering adversaries defense industrial base. Through these targeted investments, the EDU aims to strengthen national security, promote economic resilience, and counter the strategic influence of global competitors. The FY 2027 mandatory funds continue efforts from FY 2026 to make critical investments in the defense industrial base. The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Research, Development, Test & Evaluation, EDU, includes $0 thousand of discretionary and $266,450 thousand of mandatory for a total of $266,450 thousand. programElements.mission: The FY 2027 request for Drone Dominance includes $1,000,000 thousand of discretionary and $53,599,650 thousand of mandatory for a total of $54,599,650 thousand. The mandatory funds Drone Dominance which is in support of the Secretary’s Drone Dominance initiative for autonomous systems procurement, domestic production capacity, and advanced capabilities. The Drone Dominance initiative will provide investment for: 1) Contested Logistics initiatives – providing support to establish a commercially integrated logistics network capable of sustaining autonomous operations under contested conditions. 2) Collaborative Autonomy initiatives – providing support to the software, control layers, interfaces, and integration rigor required for autonomous systems to operate collaboratively at scale. 3) Force Generation initiatives – providing support for the institutions, personnel pipelines, and other resources required to operate autonomous systems at scale. 4) Counter-Unmanned Systems initiatives – providing support for defense against unmanned threats to the enterprise. 5) Asset Purchase and Industrial Demand Signal initiatives – to support the procurement of unmanned systems required to generate operational mass across air, surface, subsurface, and ground domains. The FY 2027 mandatory funds continue efforts from FY 2026 to accelerate the development and deployment of cost-effective, expendable autonomous systems for the United States Forces. The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Research, Development, Test and Evaluation, DAWG includes $0 thousand of discretionary and $225,900 thousand of mandatory for a total of $225,900 thousand.

projects.title: Defense Autonomous Warfare Group
projects.mission: The Defense Autonomous Warfare Group (DAWG) supports the Secretary of War's Drone Dominance initiative for autonomous systems procurement, domestic production capacity, and advanced capabilities. The Drone Dominance initiative will provide investment for: 1) Contested Logistics initiatives – providing support to establish a commercially integrated logistics network capable of sustaining autonomous operations under contested conditions. 2) Collaborative Autonomy initiatives – providing support to the software, control layers, interfaces, and integration rigor required for autonomous systems to operate collaboratively at scale. 3) Force Generation initiatives – providing support for the institutions, personnel pipelines, and other resources required to operate autonomous systems at scale. 4) Counter- Unmanned Systems initiatives – providing support for defense against unmanned threats to the enterprise. 5) Asset Purchase and Industrial Demand Signal initiatives – to support the procurement of unmanned systems required to generate operational mass across air, surface, subsurface, and ground domains.

programElements.mission: The Operational Energy Prototyping Fund (OEPF) addresses joint operational energy needs and requirements that enhance warfighter lethality and survivability. Modern battlefield capabilities require ever-increasing amounts of power and energy to maintain advantage, including new investment in directed energy, electronic warfare, proliferated drones, energy sensing, and artificial intelligence at the tactical edge along with traditional operational energy investment in maneuver forces’ platforms and individual warfighters. The OEPF program provides a solutions pipeline for next-generation power and energy technologies, delivering them on warfighter-relevant timelines. These solutions support current and emerging weapon systems and transition to the Services, Combatant Commands, and DIU. OEPF supports the Department's priority of rebuilding the U.S. military by creating long-term lethality and survivability advantages through an interoperable, robustly powered, and energy resilient Joint Force. OEPF investments accelerate transition of critical power and energy enablers across multiple services through an emphasis on purposeful demonstrations and validations of prototypes in operationally relevant environments. These events collect warfighter feedback, operational performance data, and focused analyses. These efforts ensure warfighters can command power across all domains. The data gathered also enables senior acquisition and programming leaders to make data-driven decisions and enact institutional change. By including service acquisition decision makers, end-user warfighters, and industry partners even before project selection, the program delivers meaningful transitions. This clear-eyed understanding of service acquisition processes has resulted in more than 80% of projects to date transitioning to service acquisition programs, industry for government procurement, or directly to operational users at the Combatant Commands. In addition to creating more transitions that endure, OEPF also accelerates these transitions by more than two years. Project selection is competitive. The program chooses projects after consultation with a wide range of stakeholders to solicit technical, programmatic, strategic, and warfighter transition inputs that maximize project impact and deconflict to avoid redundancy. OEPF prioritizes partner co-funding to increase transition results and improve affordability. Program outreach and industry partnership accelerate discovery and ensure incorporation of the latest commercial innovations and fosters and synchronizes domestic manufacturing and investments. The program regularly reviews ongoing projects for additional investment that accelerate or enhance capabilities, while evaluating any underperforming projects for early termination. PE 0604555D8Z: Operational Energy Prototyping UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 685 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 11 R-1 Line #117

projects.title: Operational Energy Prototyping
projects.mission: The Operational Energy Prototyping Fund (OEPF) provides advanced energy solutions for decisive capabilities in the expeditionary environment, like proliferated drones and tactical AI, by accelerating power and energy technology transition to the warfighter. Through rapid, warfighter-centric prototyping, OEPF de-risks acquisition and enables data-driven investment. This proven model accelerates capability delivery by more than two years and has successfully transitioned over 80% of its projects to Service acquisition programs, Combatant Commands, industry, or other partners. In addition to core program activities, OEPF also executes projects responding to specific congressional interest items (CIIs). OEPF manages these initiatives, including efforts in field-based airborne power generation, forward aerial refueling point upgrades, and laser wireless power beaming, in full alignment with its proven model. OEPF manages CIIs with the same rigor as its core projects, applying its proven principles of warfighter-centric prototyping and data-driven evaluation. This ensures these investments successfully transition meaningful capabilities to the Joint Force.

programElements.mission: This Program Element supports the Department's initiatives to Build Sustainable and Long-Term Advantage, Defend the Homeland, and Deter Aggression. The Office of the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering (OUSW(R&E)) is executing the Microelectronics Commons (the Commons) activity pursuant to the Fiscal Year (FY) 2021 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) (Pub. L. 116-283), including the CHIPS for America Act, and funded through the CHIPS for America Defense Fund established by the CHIPS Act of 2022. The FY 2021 NDAA legislation significantly emphasized solutions that promote the domestic on-shoring of capabilities to address economic and technology security concerns. Under FY 2021 NDAA Sec. 9903(b), the DoW was directed to establish a National Network for Microelectronics Research and Development (NNMRD) to enable the laboratory-to-fabrication transition of microelectronics innovations in the United States and to expand the global leadership in microelectronics of the United States. Specifically, the DoW is addressing a component of the NNMRD, the Commons, through a public- private partnership consisting of regional innovation hubs distributed across the U.S. to foster a pipeline of innovative ideas and talent residing in, for example, university labs and small business R&D teams. Background U.S. technological dominance in microelectronics materials, processes, devices, and architectural designs can only be sustained through the development of a robust domestic innovation ecosystem that fosters the rapid development and transition of novel concepts into commercially viable manufacturing processes. The U.S. innovation ecosystem has long been the driver of our nation’s technology leadership throughout the world. U.S. R&D kick-started the enormous semiconductor industry and continues to lead the world in developing the next generation of disruptive technologies including new materials, devices, circuits, architectures, and design tools. In recent years, the efficient domestic adoption of U.S. chip innovation has been threatened as emerging hardware technologies have become increasingly reliant on offshore sources for State of the Art (SOTA) manufacturing, prototyping, and investment. There are several significant hurdles that hardware startups face, including limited or expensive access to necessary facilities and design infrastructure, high costs of design intellectual property, limited expertise with hardware engineering, and PE 0604669D8Z: Microelectronics Commons - 0400D UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 697 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 8 R-1 Line #118

projects.title: Microelectronics Research Maturation
projects.mission: This project focuses on advanced prototyping activities of the Commons. Additionally, it focuses on providing cost-effective ways to capture and incentivize domestic R&D for various semiconductor technologies in a low-volume production environment and transition them for DoW and commercial market applications. Specifically, it works to transition developments from Commons Hubs resulting from technology identification and research funded by Commons PEs 0602669D8Z and matured by activities funded by Commons PE 0603669D8Z. The project also supports the establishment of the Commons Hubs, which will be networks of regional capabilities organized in collaboration with the CM to address DoW and commercial needs and requirements. The Hubs may include existing academic facilities augmented to enhance intrinsic specializations in emerging areas of microelectronics. Each Hub will concentrate on one of six technical areas including: Secure Edge Computing, 5G/6G Technology, Artificial Intelligence Hardware, Quantum Technology, Electromagnetic Warfare, and Commercial Leap Ahead Technologies. Core Facilities (i.e., fabs) are integral parts of the Hubs network that will provide key fabrication capabilities that are required to demonstrate prototypes with the volume and characteristics required to ensure reduced risk for full manufacturing production.

programElements.mission: The Office of Strategic Capital's (OSC) mission is to attract and scale capital in technology and assets that support national security. Specifically, the OSC employs unique Title 10 federal credit authorities and appropriations to crowd investment through loans, loan guarantees, and technical assistance, efficiently using taxpayer dollars that support Department of War needs. Since its authorization, the OSC has begun developing and rapidly deploying new financial products to accelerate private investment in the industrial base across Covered Technology Categories prescribed by Congress. These tools will crowd capital to secure critical supply chains and rebuild the industrial base, enhancing the U.S. competitive advantage. The FY 2027 President's Budget request supports the creation of new financial products to address a range of priorities, including greenfield projects, project finance, acquisition capital, investment fund subscription lines, and fund-level leverage to increase assets under management. These strategic capital solutions will enable the office to address key Presidential and Departmental priorities such as critical minerals, shipbuilding, infrastructure, and U.S. supply chain chokepoints.

These strategic capital solutions will enable the office to address key Presidential and Departmental priorities such as critical minerals, shipbuilding, infrastructure, and U.S. supply chain chokepoints.

programElements.mission: This program supports the Office of the Director, Cost Assessment & Program Evaluation (CAPE). It funds activities that help CAPE to implement the vision of the Deputy Secretary of War to support new approaches to campaign analysis. CAPE will accomplish this by leading studies and developing analytic tools to think about future capabilities and posture. This program provides for analytical research across a spectrum of issues and concerns. The research agenda is focused on near to long-term problems identified by the Deputy Secretary of War, and addresses difficult and complex questions linked to program alternatives for current and future capabilities and forces in order to enhance the senior leadership's deliberations and decision-making. This program provides the scientific and technical engineering services needed for research studies in the development of models and simulations and the evaluation of current analytical tools and scientific methods used to evaluate and assess future scenarios and concepts of operations for a wide range of warfighting environments and scenarios. Deliverables from this program will include reports, briefings, and analyses designed to illuminate findings and assessments. Outcomes include the compilation and campaign analyses data to support the DoW capabilities to the challenges of a near-peer warfight and strategic and programmatic options for post Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty weapons options. programElements.mission: The FY 2027 request for Joint Tactical Information Distribution System (JTIDS) includes $10,200 thousand of discretionary and $23,129 thousand of mandatory for a total of $33,329. The mandatory funds acquisition support of Combined Joint All-Domain Command and Control (CJADC2) to establish the Joint Fires Network ACAT ID program; establish a Golden Dome program office and associated program plans to deliver a national Integrated Air and Missile Defense system and accelerate acquisition of Standard Missile 3. Funds support the increasing National Intelligence major system acquisition programs. Increase in funding allows the Deputy Assistant Secretary of War Strategic, Space, and Intelligence Portfolio Management (DASW SSIPM) to fully support Presidential, Deputy Secretary of War and USW(A&S) initiatives Executive Order (EO) 14186, “The Iron (Golden) Dome for America, EO 14625 “, Modernizing Defense Acquisitions and Spurring Innovation in the Defense Industrial Base,“ Secure Enclave, Key Management Infrastructure Capability Increment 3, and Congressional direction and achieve the desired outcomes, such as strengthening program execution. This program supports the Department's initiatives to Restoring Warrior Ethos, Rebuilding Military Capabilities and Re-establishing Deterrence. This program Manages, analyzes, and informs acquisition and resourcing decisions in the nuclear modernization/acquisition; nuclear command, control, and communications (NC3); space and national intelligence (SNI); and command, control, communications, computers, and missile defense (C4MD) portfolios - C4MD: Inform synchronized modernization and delivery of new command, control, communications, and computer systems. programElements.mission: This program supports the Secretary of War priorities of rebuilding the military and reestablishing deterrence by optimizing core readiness functions to achieve peace through strength. The National Defense Strategy (NDS) for 2026 emphasizes innovation to enhance U.S. military capabilities and deter potential adversaries. This program supports the Defense Readiness Reporting System (DRRS), the single, comprehensive readiness reporting system for the Department of War mandated under Section 117, Title 10 U.S. Code. The system measures, in an objective, accurate, and timely manner, the capability of the armed forces to carry out the National Security Strategy prescribed by the President, as well as, the defense planning guidance provided by the Secretary of War, and the National Military Strategy (NMS) prescribed by the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. DRRS hosts information and applications used to support the Geographic and Functional Combatant Commanders, the Services, Combat Support Agencies, the Joint Staff and the Office of the Secretary of War. DRRS is the evolution of readiness reporting to a more comprehensive system, better able to meet the Department’s current and future readiness information challenges. Included in these challenges is the expansion in scope of the entities who can and do report readiness. Shifting from solely resource centric readiness reporting to a resource informed mission and capabilities-based reporting system, oriented towards the NMS, makes substantially more complex demands on readiness reporting, but portrays a far more relevant and holistic picture of readiness. The challenges associated with sourcing and evaluating the readiness of our forces engaged in on-going real operations, mean that force managers need applications that will query the entire Department for suitable, available organizations to meet current needs. DRRS continues to incorporate more data and develop more capable functionality to meet the evolving needs of both the operational employers of the Force, but also those responsible for Force Generation. The National Defense Authorization Act for FY 2019 revised Title 10 of U.S. Code and provided the Department direction requiring growth in the DRRS program and identified the program’s need to maintain the technical currency necessary to quickly meet future challenges associated with providing senior leaders with relevant and timely information. Such initiatives include implementing the complex data structures and visualization tools needed to operationalize the Department’s Enterprise Force Structure, reporting at lower organizational levels consistent with how Forces are employed. programElements.mission: The Rapid Defense Experimentation Reserve (RDER) program supports core program management and integration activities necessary to plan and execute a multi- year campaign of experimentation. The RDER initiative is a whole of DoW effort focused on joint experimentation to provide rapid capabilities that address our most difficult military challenges. The Secretary of War established the RDER initiative in the Defense Planning Guidance for Fiscal Years 2024-2028, to enable multi- component experimentation through a campaign of learning that accelerates technology transition and scale-up with the Services through the Deputy’s Management Action Group. This experimentation is executed using validated mission vignettes to assess Measures of Effectiveness and Measures of Performance identified in Modeling and Simulation. Integrated Assessment plans are developed for each prototype in the experiment and the data is collected through a campaign of experimentation utilizing Service demonstration venues or Combatant Command Training Exercises. The final prototype assessment will consist of a body of evidence that consists of Modeling and Simulation data, range or exercise performance, and doctrine, organization, training, materiel, leadership and education, personnel, and facilities (DOTMPLF) issues. The assessment will drive a Department decision point for transition or acceleration of RDER projects. The Office of the Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering OUSW(R&E) process for incubating promising prototypes starts with a novel experimental design based on a web of interconnected systems addressing specific Defense Planning Scenarios. This approach is unique from other experimentation that focuses on determining individual system efficacy. Additionally, the experimental design is unique in using the web of systems to explore new warfighting concepts that capitalize on contributions of multiple Services as a true joint force. The experimentation yields live data which is evaluated to depict system operational analysis against the predicted performance from modeling and simulation. The Operational Utility Assessment establishes the body of evidence for the "best of breed" and the rapid adaptation of the capability by the Services. OUSW(R&E) manages the Department’s multiple RDER experimentation events and conducts integration activities with the Joint Force. OUSW(R&E) oversees an integrated selection process with the Joint Staff, Services, and Combatant Commands that incorporates the most promising innovative prototypes into existing exercise venues such as: Balikatan and Grey Flag. RDER utilizes funding to plan, integrate, and oversee joint experiments; provide assessments on project viability; and, deliver results to facilitate decisions on transitioning promising capabilities with the Services. projects.title: Rapid Defense Experimentation
projects.mission: The RDER program focuses on a campaign of joint experimentation on rapid capabilities that address our most complex military challenges. The Secretary of War established RDER in the Defense Planning Guidance to enable multi-component joint experimentation through a campaign of learning and to establish a body of evidence to support rapid adoption by the Services. RDER enables the Services, agencies, industry, and other organizations to identify “best of breed” capabilities to be prototyped in large-scale joint experiments and validate and/or refine the Joint Warfighting Concept (JWC). OUSW(R&E)’s process for incubating promising prototypes starts with a novel experimental design based on a web of interconnected systems addressing specific Defense Planning Scenarios. This approach is unique from other experimentation that focuses on determining individual system efficacy. Additionally, the experimental design is unique in using the web of systems to explore new warfighting concepts that capitalize on contributions of multiple Services as a true joint force. The experimentation yields live data that the OUSW(R&E) analyzes against the performance predicted in modelling and simulation. OUSW(R&E) manages the Department’s multiple RDER experimentation events and conducts integration activities with the Joint Force. OUSW(R&E) oversees an integrated selection process with the Joint Staff, Services, and Combatant Commands that incorporates the most promising innovative prototypes into existing exercise venues such as: Project Capstone, Northern Edge, Valiant Shield, Talisman Sabre, Grey Flag, Joint Battle Problem, and TREX. RDER utilizes funding to plan, integrate, and oversee joint experiments; provide assessments on project viability; and deliver the results to the Deputy Secretary of War management action group to facilitate decisions on transitioning promising capabilities with the Services.

programElements.mission: The Office of the Deputy Assistant Secretary of War (DASW) for Multi-Domain Joint Operations (MDJO) serves as the primary driver for identifying, developing, and transitioning high-priority joint capabilities to the warfighter. In accordance with Public Law 116-283 and DoD Directive 7045.20, MDJO collaborates across the Department of War to ensure the seamless integration of cutting-edge technologies into operational use. Key efforts executed include: •Mission Engineering and Integration Activity (MEIA): Plans and supports the execution of mission engineering, industry engagement, experimentation, and capability assessments to address priority Joint Operational Problems (JOPs). Establishes Mission Capability and Analysis Teams (MCATs) and informs the Requirements and Resourcing Alignment Board (RRAB). •JROC Representation: Serves as the official Research and Engineering (R&E) representative to the Joint Requirements Oversight Council (JROC), providing technical expertise to shape requirements, concepts of operation, and solutions for urgent operational needs. •Senior Governance Engagement: Actively participates in senior governance forums, including Cost Assessment and Program Evaluation (CAPE) reviews and Warfighter Senior Integration Groups (W-SIGs), to ensure the integration of innovative technologies into decision-making processes. •Technology Modernization Transition Reviews (TMTRs): Executes TMTRs in collaboration with the Joint Staff, Acquisition and Sustainment (A&S), and other stakeholders to accelerate technology options for JROC-designated high-priority operational problems. •Joint Lethality Engineering (JLE): Conducts critical research and analysis to address the nation's most pressing military challenges. By enhancing the lethality and survivability of our systems against advanced adversarial threats, JLE directly contributes to the Department's investment decisions and the overall effectiveness of the joint force. This program element spans all security levels and warfighting domains, ensuring a comprehensive approach to bolstering our military capabilities. PE 0604791D8Z: Multi-Domain Joint Operations (MDJO) UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 733 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 9 R-1 Line #122

projects.title: Tech Modernization Transition
projects.mission: This project supports identification and transition of priority joint capabilities to the Military Services and Agencies, through persistent, proactive stakeholder engagement with continuous transition risk assessment, development of technology transition plans, and coordination across the Department of War. Project identification activities inform the specific focus areas for the Assistant of Secretary of War (ASW) Mission Capabilities for Mission Engineering and Prototyping & Experimentation activities. Concurrently, project transition activities accelerate the fielding of successfully demonstrated joint capabilities. MDJO’s core mission is to align Joint Force efforts to the interim National Defense Strategy priorities. Additionally, this project funds TMTR which are executed to review the health of research and development portfolios to identify promising technologies to address warfighter gaps and support investment decisions. In FY 2026, this project also supports MEIA, the DoW's new enterprise framework and approach for delivering capability at speed to the Joint Force. MEIA integrates mission engineering, rapid experimentation, operational integration, and solution refinement with industry.

programElements.mission: The FY 2027 request for Joint Energetics Transition Office (JETO) includes $6,278 thousand of discretionary and $500,000 thousand of mandatory for a total of $506,278 thousand. The FY 2027 $500,000 thousand of mandatory funds continue efforts from FY 2026 to conduct systems engineering and rapid, full-scale prototyping of weapons systems and supporting components to demonstrate the technical maturity of new energetic materials technologies. It will sponsor the expedited maturation, validation, verification, and accreditation of Modeling and Simulation tools. It will conduct modernized Test and Evaluation activities to support the systems engineering and modeling and simulation missions, ultimately encouraging the development of increased fidelity and enhanced throughput for future qualification work. The FY 2026 spend plan amount for Joint Energetics Transition Office (JETO) includes $2,986 thousand of discretionary and $1,000 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $3,986 thousand. The FY 2024 National Defense Authorizations Act (NDAA) directs The Under Secretary of War for Acquisition and Sustainment (USW(A&S)) and Under Secretary of War for Research and Engineering (USW(R&E)) to select the Head and Deputy Head of Joint Energetics Transition Office (JETO), respectively. JETO will establish and manage a DoW field activity dedicated to systems engineering associated with energetic materials. Additionally, JETO has been assigned strategic roles, tactical roles, coordination responsibilities, and directed studies. This new joint office is intended to improve the transition of energetic materials and technologies to programs of record. It will also result in cost savings through economies of scale and lower barriers for additional suppliers entering the market. Roles and responsibilities of include: DoW Field Office: The establishment and management of a Department of War field activity will be dedicated to systems engineering associated with energetic materials. The field activity mission will be to reduce technical risk; integrate research; develop, test, and evaluate energetic materials; and perform Department of War system demonstration programs on novel energetic materials for use in weapon systems. This field office will execute studies to inform the Energetic Materials Strategic Plan and Investment Strategy. Per NDAA language, these activities must be funded under budget activity 3 (advanced technology development) or budget activity 4 (advanced component development and prototypes). Strategic Roles: JETO will create an Energetic Materials Strategic Plan and Investment Strategy and report on changes and progress annually. PE 0604797D8Z: Joint Energetics Transition Office (JETO... programElements.mission: This program exploits advances in technology to develop, improve and implement detection, information processing and decision support for biosurveillance. The mission of this program is to create a fit-for-purpose biosurveillance capability with federated collection, processing, analysis, production, and dissemination capabilities to enable strategic, anticipatory, and operational decision-making for deterrence and risk mitigation. To accomplish this mission, the Biosurveillance Program will: 1) Implement agile planning and response processes through a cross-functional biosurveillance organizational architecture—with the structure, function, and authorities —to support time-sensitive needs of DoW leaders; 2) Expand physical and digital detection capabilities and capacity—infused with intelligence collection—to enable early warning, sustained awareness, and agile response focusing on advanced technological capabilities; 3) Enhance biosurveillance insights by increasing data value, knowledge generation and accelerating processing of existing health and biodefense repositories into a fit-for purpose analytic capability; 4) Communicate the full range of analytic products necessary for leadership decision-making across the continuum of conflict. This budget is justified by the critical gap, identified in the 2023 Biodefense Posture Review, in the Department's ability to gather and analyze timely biothreat information to protect the Joint Force and enhance national security. The funding will establish this necessary capability by operationalizing a Biosurveillance Information Platform (BIP), expanding next-generation sequencing, and improving data sharing. These investments are essential for enhancing readiness and resilience against a dynamic landscape of natural, accidental, and deliberate biological threats.
