projects.title: Industrial Base Analysis and
projects.mission: Global supply chain disruptions have become more common, with recent world events highlighting risks and vulnerabilities that undermine our national security. The 2022 Industrial Capabilities Report (ICR) report outlined strategic focus areas and enabling capabilities, associated vulnerabilities, and recommendations to strengthen the DIB. The FY 2027 IBAS budget request reflects the DoW’s commitment to ensuring our supply chains can provide our warfighters with decisive advantage. This budget includes investments to respond to Executive Orders, emerging and modernization priorities and technologies, and other defense requirements. The IBAS budget is the result of significant coordination for each strategic focus area via the Deputy’s Management Action Group (DMAG), which developed an integrated and prioritized investment strategy to address the most pressing needs for each focus area, to include mapping to investment authorities. The FY 2027 IBAS budget request reflects the outcome of the DMAG recommendations and has been coordinated to complement adjacent investments by related programs including the Defense Production Act Title III and Manufacturing Technology (ManTech) programs, and within the Military Services. Accordingly, investments in the following strategic focus areas will establish, sustain, and expand domestic capabilities and capacities to build more sustainable and resilient supply chains. Strategic & Critical Minerals - Nearly every DoW weapons system, information technology resource, and supply chain — from cutting-edge missile defense systems to petroleum refining — relies on critical minerals and materials. These include, but are not limited to, rare earth elements (REEs), germanium, vanadium, titanium, lithium, cobalt, gallium, and specialized alloys essential for various defense applications. In particular, the midstream minerals processing is heavily controlled by single nations for numerous materials. For example, a significant portion of global REE processing is concentrated in a single nation as well as other critical materials necessary for microelectronics manufacturing. A disruption in any critical mineral or material supply chain, whether due to world events or planned for geopolitical reasons, significantly affects the DoW’s ability to manufacture a wide range of platforms and equipment and is thus a serious risk to national security. A resilient supply chain for critical mineral oxides, metals, and specialized alloys is required to enable continued manufacture and sustainment of DoW systems. To provide domestically made specialized alloys and materials, the entire critical mineral supply chain must be supported: from mining ores, to processing and enriching the ores, to extraction of individual mineral oxides, to metallization, to creation of specialized alloys using domestic metals, and finally to qualification of domestic alloys and materials in end-use defense applications. The DoW must also be ready to address shortfalls in other elements such as gallium, scandium, noble gases, titanium, vanadium, germanium and other materials used in traditional defense systems, microelectronics, hypersonics, specialized electronics manufacturing processes which will require additional funding. To mitigate the most pressing critical mineral supply chain risks, the DoW will utilize FY 2026 funding to fund a domestic metallization capability for critical materials. This key supply chain node must be addressed to enable the manufacture of specialized alloys and materials from domestically processed metals and for use in other specialty applications. Efforts will enable reshoring various mineral processing capabilities to support the aluminum and zinc industries as well for specialty by-products including but not limited to gallium, germanium, vanadium, and tungsten. The department will need to be agile and respond to emerging priorities as supply chain PE 0607210D8Z: Industrial Base Analysis and Sustainment... programElements.mission: Counterproliferation (CP) Advanced Development prioritizes investments aligned to the Defense Planning Guidance (DPG) approved on August 7, 2025, and supports the 2026 National Defense Strategy (NDS) objective of peace through strength with advanced development of capabilities that reestablish deterrence and rebuild our military. Program investments in CP capabilities, enable the Department to maintain credible options to defend the homeland against WMD use. Investments result in capabilities that take action to neutralize nuclear, chemical, biological and radiological materials of concern (2026 NDS). This program modernizes currently fielded capabilities for operating units with assigned CP/CWMD missions to disrupt, degrade, delay, deny, and defeat adversary nuclear, chemical, and biological weapons, delivery platforms, command and control systems, and hardened and deeply buried targets. Program investments enable modernized WMD deterrence and CP capabilities that provide flexible options across all domains, including tactical situational awareness, low visibility methods of maneuver, and the ability to employ immediate effects without diminishing future capabilities, enabling active campaigning that supports the Administration’s CP priorities, mitigating escalatory risk. In accordance with the DPG, funding is prioritized for projects that close Joint Force warfighter capability gaps to defend territory along the First Island Chain (FIC) and the western hemisphere. An annual investment strategy is used to meet emergent operational needs validated by the Joint Force, yielding new fielded capabilities within one to two years. This program funds labor, materials, and travel in support of its requirements, performed by a government agency or by private individuals or organizations under a contract with the government, for activities and acquisitions including Research, Development, Test & Evaluation (RDT&E), assessments and analyses, education, and other activities related to capability development and fielding of bio-deterrence, nuclear modernization, homeland missile defense, one-way attack autonomous systems, and maritime/FIC territory superiority. PE 0607310D8Z: Counterproliferation Modernization UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 1259 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 6 R-1 Line #234

programElements.peName: Radiological and Nuclear Defense Modernization Operational System
programElements.mission: This program supports the DoW’s priorities to Rebuild our Military and Reestablish Deterrence and supports the Office of the Under Secretary of War for Acquisition and Sustainment (OUSW(A&S)) Radiological and Nuclear Defense Capability Development (RNDCD) Program. An annual investment strategy is used to meet emergent operational needs which are validated by the Joint Force and yield fielded capabilities within one to three years. The program supports the modernization of radiological and nuclear (R/N) defense capabilities that enable the Department to prevent R/N attacks on the homeland and the Joint Force abroad through integrated deterrence. The program provides the Joint Forces the tools needed to survive and operate in and through a R/N environment through networked R/N detection, indication, and identification capabilities in support of the National Defense Strategy’s deterrence by denial strategy and prioritization of the Nuclear Deterrence Posture within the FY2027 Defense Planning Guidance. This program funds labor, materials, and travel to support the requirements of this program, performed by a government agency or by private individuals or organizations under a contract with the government, for activities and acquisitions including Research, Development, Test, and Evaluation (RDT&E), assessments and analyses, research studies, education, and other activities related to capability development and fielding. programElements.peName: Enterprise Platform and Capabilities Software Pilot Program
programElements.mission: This program funds capabilities and activities to make disparate DoW datasets more widely accessible, understandable, interoperable, and actionable across the DoW enterprise by providing the enterprise data stack, analytic tools, and data engineering expertise to improve DoW decision making across multiple functional areas and organization levels. Furthermore, these foundational investments are essential to enable advanced AI inference, allowing for the operational deployment of machine learning models to generate predictive insights and accelerate decision-making. This includes enhancing user platform and data, increasing the number of data sources, improving data quality, and driving enablement across the DoW workforce to effectively leverage the data and analytic capabilities provided through War Data Platform. PE 0608140D8Z: Enterprise Platform and Capabilities Sof... UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 1331 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 19 R-1 Line #312

projects.title: War Data Platform Infrastructure
projects.mission: This program funds capabilities and activities to make disparate DoW datasets more widely accessible, understandable, interoperable, and actionable across the Department of War (DoW) enterprise by providing the government-owned, contractor operated enterprise data stack, analytic tools, and data engineering expertise to improve DoW decision making across multiple functional areas and organization levels. This includes speeding data access, increasing the number of data sources, improving data quality and driving enablement across the DoW workforce to effectively leverage the data and analytic capabilities provided through War Data Platform.

projects.title: Business Mission Analytics
projects.mission: he Business & Mission Analytics project enables DoW organizations to better leverage data, analytics, and artificial intelligence capabilities to improve decision making in multiple functional and cross-functional areas. Through the delivery of enterprise capabilities, services, and best practices, we empower business and warfighting operators to leverage data to achieve decision advantage. Funding continues to sustain existing products that supports customers across the Department, including: the Joint Staff, Combatant Commands, OSW Principal Staff Assistants, and cross-service efforts. This is comprised of 4 major areas: (1) The War Data Platform Program Office manages our centralized budget, (2) Support acquisition management, (3) Program considerations and documentation, (4) Contracts management and agile management. Product teams provide centralized customer support, data discovery, user onboarding, drive accountability of product and technical roadmaps via increment planning and WDP metrics, provide platform access, training and enablement for the entire user base and upskill the workforce in accessing and using WDP’s data and AI capabilities. Principal Staff Assistant (PSA) Support is an enabler to ensure seamless integration of data and automation products into decision-making capability. They include support for data scientists, data engineers and data product support for tools to design and build high-quality products for executive customers with enterprise-level impact. It enables CDAO to support new and existing customers by enhancing our operating model and business practices to deliver high quality data products to any tool, application or environment across the Department, thereby driving enterprise scaling that is platform and tool agnostic. The Combatant Command Embedded Support (CES) portfolio is an enabler of operational success and national security objectives, directly supporting the Department of War’s (DoW) ability to execute strategic operations, maintain regional stability, and adapt to dynamic global challenges. By providing centralized management of contracting digital talent labor embedded across all Combatant Commands, this portfolio ensures seamless integration of data capabilities into mission-critical processes on both the War Data Platform, Maven Smart System and other platforms as required for mission success.

projects.title: SUNet
projects.mission: The Secure Unclassified Network (SUNet) provides critical infrastructure for fast-moving AI development missions, Interagency Task Force groups, and experimental DoW missions that cannot be met by other existing capabilities. SUNet is the Department’s only one off Department of Defense Information Network (DODIN), Government-owned (GOTS) network and compute platform that is authorized to support data and missions up to the CUI level. SUNet adheres to DoW Risk Management Framework standards and is accessible anywhere in the world with internet. The platform acts as a sandbox and provides segmented, customizable areas of compute and storage for DoW and mission partners to rapidly onboard, collaborate, develop, and validate cutting edge commercial software and tools. Mission owners across the Department rely on SUNet’s flexible and accessible infrastructure to achieve their AI/ML missions and maintain operational advantage.

projects.title: Data Reform Initiative (AWG)
projects.mission: The Analysis Working Group (AWG) assists the Secretary and Deputy Secretary of War in guiding the analytic community to ensure analysis products are robust and of the highest quality. The end goal is an analytic enterprise that is agile, responsive, and provides sound decision support for the Secretary and Deputy Secretary. Funding for this project supports analytic research across the Department to guide reform of the Department’s analytic enterprise. Projects that support this effort help develop a high performing and innovative analytic enterprise with the right policies, structures, people, and tools to support timely strategic decisions that create an advantage for the U.S Military now and into the future.

programElements.mission: The Department of War (DoW) relies on a multitude of mission-critical modeling and simulation (M&S) codes and tools that are utilized across the munitions, platforms and warfighter communities. This program’s mission is for the sustainment of these M&S codes to keep them functioning at appropriate levels to support key DoW mission spaces. M&S codes have been previously developed by OUSW(R&E) and DoW programs and are continuously and incrementally improved and enhanced adding new capabilities that support evolving mission criteria. Enhancements and improvements are commonly distributed in new releases and versions of the software and are increasingly more adaptable to digital engineering frameworks that enable Machine Learning (ML) and automated workflows. M&S codes enable detailed design and performance assessments, augment physical testing to improve system performance and reduce costs, and support weapons employment and mission planning. Sustainment of the M&S codes includes several activities to ensure they are functioning and executing at efficient and optimal levels. Compatibility testing of new releases to assess proper functionality on DoW systems, including within the DoW High Performance Computing ecosystem. Installation and configuration support for compatibility with DoW system requirements. Debugging and troubleshooting user problems, and direct support from the software developers for complex use cases. Developing and updating training materials for efficient implementation with users. Thorough review of the source code for new releases to ensure any sensitive content is stripped, while remaining intelligible. PE 0608500D8Z: Weaponeering Code Sustainment UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 1351 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 6 R-1 Line #313

programElements.mission: Acquisition Visibility supports the Department’s effort to implement Executive Order 14265 “Modernizing Defense Acquisitions and Spurring Innovation in the Defense Industrial Base.” By leveraging machine learning, artificial intelligence (AI) and other digital tools, Acquisition Visibility will rapidly and iteratively transform the Department’s acquisition digital systems and processes with an emphasis on speed, flexibility, and execution. To achieve this, Acquisition Visibility will build a joint multi-cloud enterprise digital infrastructure for hosting digital platforms and AI tools; provide data engineering, data science, and digital governance expertise to build enterprise data pipelines and develop data governance standards; develop and deploy production joint data integration platform(s) and Department-wide acquisition visibility applications(s); and operational and algorithmic test and evaluation expertise and tools. The FY 2027 request for Acquisition Visibility - Software Pilot Program includes $64,364 thousand of discretionary and $109,176 thousand of mandatory for a total of $173,540 thousand. Acquisition Visibility mandatory funding for $109,176 thousand will allow the Department to scale efforts to leverage digital and AI tools within the acquisition system to reduce administrative burden and plan acquisitions that deliver weapons faster than our adversaries. It will automate the preparation, verification, and approval processes of portfolio/program strategies to enable acquisition professionals to focus on capability delivery and mission outcomes, while simultaneously ensuring greater speed and quality. The funding will also support risk-informed and mission-based analysis and kill chain analysis for mission threads. PE 0608648D8Z: Acquisition Visibility - Software Pilot ... programElements.peName: Accelerate the Procurement and Fielding of Innovative Technologies
programElements.mission: As directed by the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) FY 2025 Sec. 861, the Accelerate the Procurement and Fielding of Innovative Technologies (APFIT) program competitively selects projects in coordination with the Service Acquisition Executives and the Joint Staff. Project selection is guided by the National Defense Strategy with a focus on building enduring advantages through making the right technology investments and strengthening the resiliency and adaptability in our industrial base. APFIT conducts a merit-based, internal Department of War (DoW) process to solicit, evaluate, and select projects for $10 million - $50 million of procurement funding with priority to those provided by small businesses and non-traditional defense contractors. This program helps deliver capabilities to the Services and Agencies by providing critical procurement funding to programs with production-ready capability and a current procurement funding disconnect, thereby enabling the transition of the program across the "valley of death".

projects.title: Accelerate the Procurement and
projects.mission: The Accelerate the Procurement and Fielding of Innovative Technologies (APFIT) program is authorized in Section 861 of the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) for FY 2025. APFIT is a competitive, merit-based internal DoW process to solicit, evaluate and select projects for $10 million to $50 million of procurement funding, with priority to those provided by small businesses and non-traditional defense innovators. APFIT accelerates the delivery of these capabilities to the Services and Agencies by providing critical procurement funding to programs with production-ready capability and a current procurement funding disconnect, thereby enabling the transition of the program across the "valley of death".

programElements.mission: The Defense Innovation Unit (DIU) mission is to strengthen U.S. national security by accelerating the adoption of commercial technology and externally developed products connecting American entrepreneurs and commercial technology companies to Department needs at speed. DIU serves as the Department's center of excellence for commercial technology adoption and product transition, available to Portfolio Acquisition Executives and program offices across the Department. DIU is the Department's primary interface with the nontraditional and commercial ecosystem for rapid product adoption and transition. In support of the Secretary of War’s directive to transform the Defense Innovation Ecosystem to Accelerate Warfighting Advantage, DIU will: 1) Focus on high technologies that produce overmatch domination; 2) Focus on technologies that reduce the number of Soldiers, Sailors, Airmen, Marines, and Guardians in harm’s way; 3) Focus on capabilities that can scale quickly and dramatically reduce costs. Funding in this Program Element is requested to support agile research, development, test and evaluation, procurement, production, modification, and operation and maintenance requirements, including the initial acquisition of end-items for operational use. Program Element 0608776D8Z executes the Department's requirement under two project codes beginning in FY 2026: 496 - DIU Fielding and 613 - Support to COCOMs. Project Code 496: Supports the agile research, development, test and evaluation, procurement, production, modification, and operation and maintenance requirements, including the initial acquisition of end-items for operational use. programElements.mission: In support of the Department of War, Office of Strategic Capital’s (OSC) mission is to attract and scale private capital to technologies critical to the national and economic security of the United States. To accomplish its mission, OSC uses loans, loan guarantees, and technical assistance – an efficient use of taxpayer dollars – to crowd in investment by aligning government and private sector incentives. The office’s tools take advantage of the power of markets and competition to support DoW needs. OSC’s investments are guided by: (i) control of economic chokepoints; (ii) scaling of key industries, and (iii) commercialization of critical technologies. OSC investments will provide the Department of War advantages in all these competitive arenas, especially by securing critical supply chains and rebuilding the industrial base. Specifically, OSC will: • Develop new financial products, such as direct equipment finance loans for manufacturers, project finance loans to support capital intensive projects, working capital loans for businesses in need of short-term financing, and loan guarantees through private banks. • Provide loans, loan guarantees and technical assistance to businesses, funds, and other entities within the 33 Covered Technology Categories prescribed by Congress. • Identify and prioritize areas for investment through engagements with the Services, Combatant Commands, National Security Council, and other interagency partners. • Create new data tools to map economic networks to determine chokepoints that impact U.S. national and economic security. • Increase private capital investment to enhance the commercialization of DoW and United States Government (USG) science & technology investments. PE 0901579D8Z: Office of Strategic Capital (OSC) UNCLASSIFIED Volume 3 - 777 Office of the Secretary Of Defense Page 1 of 9 R-1 Line #141
